region_of_origin: United States (globally distributed; served in Korean restaurant context in Singapore)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Coca-Cola is approximately 330 ml (one can), equivalent to about 346 g, as commonly served in Singapore and food courts.

nutrient_source.title: Beverages, carbonated, cola, contains caffeine — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 174851)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for regular cola carbonated beverage with caffeine. Values are per 100 g (approximately 100 ml given density ~1.04 g/ml). This is the standard reference for Coca-Cola Classic nutrient composition.

gi_source.title: Coca-Cola, regular — Glycemic Index Database, University of Sydney (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, 2008)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 63 for regular Coca-Cola is reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (63 × 36.7) / 100 ≈ 23.1, where carbohydrate per 346 g serving = 10.6 × 3.46 = 36.7 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (4 mg per 100 g); primary concern is high sugar intake and potential weight gain contributing to elevated blood pressure with regular consumption
hypertension.guidance: Sodium content in Coca-Cola is negligible and does not directly raise blood pressure; however, regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with weight gain and insulin resistance, which are independent risk factors for hypertension, and should be limited in hypertensive individuals.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High free sugar content (10.6 g per 100 g; ~36.7 g per 330 ml serving) with medium glycemic index of 63, producing a high glycemic load of approximately 23 per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Coca-Cola delivers a large bolus of rapidly absorbed free sugars with a glycemic load of approximately 23 per standard serving, causing significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid regular cola beverages and substitute with water, unsweetened tea, or diet alternatives.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High free sugar intake from regular consumption is associated with elevated triglycerides, increased visceral adiposity, and pro-inflammatory pathways linked to cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened cola beverages has been associated in prospective cohort studies with increased risk of cardiovascular events; individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit intake and prefer unsweetened beverages to reduce added sugar load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Coca-Cola (commonly called Coke) is a globally iconic carbonated soft drink originally developed in the United States in 1886 by pharmacist John Pemberton. It is manufactured by The Coca-Cola Company and is one of the most widely consumed beverages in the world. The drink is characterised by its distinctive sweet, slightly acidic flavour profile derived from a proprietary blend of natural flavours, caramel colour, phosphoric acid, and caffeine dissolved in carbonated water sweetened with sucrose or high-fructose corn syrup depending on the market. In Singapore, Coca-Cola is served across all cuisine types including Korean fried chicken restaurants, where it is a popular pairing with crispy fried chicken and beer. A standard 330 ml serving contains approximately 36.7 g of free sugars and 142 kcal, with negligible protein, fat, fibre, and micronutrients. The glycemic index is 63 (medium) and the glycemic load per serving is approximately 23, making it a high-glycemic-load beverage. Nutritionally, Coca-Cola provides energy almost entirely from simple sugars with no beneficial micronutrients. It is classified as an ultra-processed beverage. Clinically, regular consumption is associated with increased risk of Type 2 Diabetes, obesity, dental caries, and cardiovascular disease. It is suitable for vegetarians and vegans and is gluten-free and dairy-free. In Korean dining contexts in Singapore, it is often listed on menus as 'Coke' or 'CB1 Coke' as a standard beverage option.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a can of Coke?
What is the sugar content of Coca-Cola?
Can I drink Coke if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of Coca-Cola?
How much sugar is in a 330ml Coke?
meal_context: beverage pairing with Korean fried chicken
fast food and casual dining beverage

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 174851) for regular cola carbonated beverage with caffeine, which is an exact match for Coca-Cola Classic. GI value of 63 sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values. Both are approved primary sources under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: Protein_g is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central — contains no protein by product formulation. Fat_g is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central — contains no fat by product formulation. Saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg are all confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. Dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero — naturally zero as this is a sugar-water carbonated beverage with no plant fibre. Vitamin values (vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg) are confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. Glycemic index of 63 places Coca-Cola in the medium GI category (56–69 inclusive per Veritas threshold rules). Glycemic load per 330 ml serving calculated as (63 × 36.7) / 100 = 23.1. The food is listed as 'CB1. Coke' on the field survey menu of O Chicken and Beer Korean Fried Chicken, Boat Quay, Singapore; the canonical food name has been normalised to 'Coca-Cola (Coke)'. Korean local name 코카콜라 is the standard Korean transliteration used in South Korea and Korean-context menus. Chinese names 可口可乐 (simplified) and 可口可樂 (traditional) are the official registered Chinese brand names used by The Coca-Cola Company. Malay, Tamil, and Hindi names and aliases have been added to meet the Singaporean multilingual requirements, as the food is sourced from field survey data and served in a Singaporean context. Price range SGD 4.00 preserved from input. This record covers regular full-sugar Coca-Cola only; diet, zero-sugar, and flavoured variants are separate food records.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Drafted full record. Identified need to verify zero-macro justification phrases in notes for protein_g and fat_g.

Iteration 2: Added explicit confirmed zero justification phrases for all zero nutrient values in notes field. Added Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names and aliases to address G6, Q1, and Q8 failures, interpreting the validator's feedback as requiring Singaporean multilingual coverage due to the food's source and context. Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases consistency. Confirmed GI=63 correctly classified as medium (56–69). All hard gates now pass. Score 8/8 achieved.
